---
title: "Introduction to Options"
date: "2026-06-01"
categories: [foundations, options]
description: "A primer on options contracts — what they are, how they work, and why they matter to quantitative finance."
bibliography: references.bib
nocite: '@*'
---

Options are among the most versatile instruments in modern finance. This post introduces the key concepts needed to understand how options work before we get into pricing theory.

## What is an Option?

An option is a financial derivative that gives the holder the right, but not the obligation, to buy or sell an underlying asset at a specified price on or before a given date. The price paid for this right is called the **premium**.

## Calls and Puts

There are two fundamental types of options:

- A **call option** gives the holder the right to *buy* the underlying at the strike price
- A **put option** gives the holder the right to *sell* the underlying at the strike price

Each can be either bought (long) or sold (short), giving four basic positions.

## Key Terminology

Before diving into pricing, it helps to be precise about the core vocabulary: the **strike price** $K$, the **expiration date** $T$, the **underlying price** $S_t$, and the **risk-free rate** $r$. These four quantities appear in virtually every options model we will encounter.

## References

::: {#refs}
:::

<hr class="revision-divider">
<details>
<summary>Revision History</summary>

- June 2026 — First published

</details>
